narrative: As a Black male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and an LSAT score of 28, both below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among past students in our dataset. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 76.7% chance that you would fail it— a threshold that led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed right away. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged heavily on your academic performance in school, particularly your rankings among peers. The most influential factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your class that year, far below the average of around the middle of the pack. This low standing signaled to the model a potential struggle with the rigorous demands of legal studies leading up to the bar. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile of 1, again at the very bottom compared to the typical mid-range performance, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic momentum from the start. Your undergraduate GPA offered a slight counterbalance, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing since it was close enough to average to show some foundational strength, though not enough to offset the other signals. Compared to students who typically pass the bar, your early and later school rankings were notably weaker, which amplified the model's caution despite your full-time commitment aligning with the vast majority of successful applicants. We understand this outcome may feel disheartening, especially given the barriers many Black men from middle-income backgrounds face in competitive fields like law. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your grades in any ongoing studies to climb those decile rankings, and perhaps seeking targeted bar prep resources or mentorship programs designed for underrepresented students. Building this stronger profile could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor for other schools. With persistence, you're positioning yourself for the success you deserve.
